footer.description:
Free BAC calculator powered by the Widmark formula.
No ads. No login. No data collected.
footer.disclaimer: This BAC calculator is provided for educational purposes only. It is not medical or legal advice. Never use an app to decide whether to drive after drinking. If you need help with alcohol use, contact SAMHSA at 1-800-662-4357.

landing.title: BAC Calculator for fast, educational blood alcohol estimates.
landing.description: This BAC Calculator uses the Widmark formula to estimate blood alcohol content, compare BAC estimates against common legal thresholds, and show how long alcohol may take to clear.
landing.calculatorDisclaimer: Food, medication, genetics, and tolerance can all change your real BAC estimate.
cardLines: This BAC Calculator is an estimate, not a breathalyzer or medical test.
Never use a BAC Calculator to decide whether to drive after drinking.
landing.homeSubtitle: Free BAC Calculator for a fast blood alcohol content estimate. Enter a few basics, get a quick educational result in seconds, and move to the full calculator when you need country legal limits or more drink types.
landing.homeHowBody: This BAC Calculator uses the Widmark formula to estimate blood alcohol content from your weight, biological sex, number of drinks, and time since your first drink. The homepage version is a quick BAC Calculator built for speed, so it focuses on the most important inputs first. It gives you a fast educational estimate, not a breathalyzer reading or medical test. If you need a deeper blood alcohol content breakdown, the full BAC Calculator includes more drink types, legal context, and a more detailed result view.
landing.homeLegalTitle: BAC Calculator Legal Limits by Country
landing.homeLegalBody: Blood alcohol content laws vary widely. In the United States and Canada, the standard adult driving limit is often 0.08%, while countries like China and Sweden use 0.02%, and some places use zero tolerance rules. The full BAC Calculator lets you compare your estimate against country-specific thresholds and review the legal context in one place. A legal BAC does not guarantee safe driving, but the full BAC Calculator makes it easier to understand where your estimate stands.

quickCalculator.cardDescription: Answer five quick questions and get a fast BAC estimate with less friction.
quickCalculator.disclaimer: Educational estimate only. Never use to decide whether to drive.
quickCalculator.fullCalculator: Full calculator - country legal limits, more drink types ->
quickCalculator.resultPlaceholder: Enter your drinks, weight, and time, then tap calculate to see your BAC estimate.

calculator.title: How the BAC Calculator Works
calculator.description: Use the full BAC Calculator below to estimate blood alcohol content by country, drink type, body weight, biological sex, and time elapsed. This BAC Calculator applies the Widmark formula so you can compare your estimated BAC against the selected legal limit and see how long alcohol may take to clear.
calculator.panelDescription: Choose your country, enter your drink type, add your body weight, and let the BAC Calculator estimate blood alcohol content and compare it with the selected legal limit.
calculator.drinksError: Please enter the number of drinks.

levelDescriptions.minimal: Slight relaxation. Below most legal limits.
levelDescriptions.mild: Approaching the legal limit. Judgment may be affected.
levelDescriptions.impaired: Above the common legal limit. Significant impairment.
levelDescriptions.severe: Dangerous BAC. Major coordination loss is likely.
levelDescriptions.danger: Potentially life-threatening. Seek medical help immediately.

table.title: What Your BAC Level Really Means
table.description: Blood alcohol content measures grams of alcohol per deciliter of blood. This BAC Calculator table adds context to the BAC estimate you see after using the calculator.

rows.effects: Slight relaxation and mild mood lift. Reaction time is largely unaffected. Below legal limits in all jurisdictions.
Reduced inhibition and subtly impaired judgment. Fine motor control begins to decline. At or near the 0.08% US legal limit.
Noticeable balance, coordination, and reaction impairment. Slurred speech and memory gaps become more common.
Major loss of coordination and balance. Nausea and disorientation are common. Blackout risk rises sharply.
Risk of alcohol poisoning, loss of consciousness, and respiratory suppression. Seek emergency medical attention immediately.

factors.title: Factors That Affect Your BAC
factors.description: No two people metabolize alcohol identically. This BAC Calculator uses the Widmark formula, but real-world BAC outcomes still depend on several personal factors.

items.title: Body Weight
items.body: Heavier individuals have a larger volume of body fluids, which dilutes alcohol more efficiently than in lighter individuals.

items.title: Biological Sex
items.body: Differences in body composition and alcohol dehydrogenase levels can change how alcohol distributes through the body.

items.title: Time Elapsed
items.body: Your liver metabolizes alcohol over time, usually around 0.015% BAC per hour on average.

items.title: Food Intake
items.body: Eating before or during drinking slows absorption, which can reduce and delay peak BAC.

items.title: Medications
items.body: Prescription and over-the-counter drugs can amplify impairment or change how your body responds to alcohol.

items.title: Genetics & Tolerance
items.body: Genetic variation changes metabolism speed. Tolerance can affect how impaired you feel, but not your actual BAC.

faq.title: BAC Calculator FAQ
faq.description: Common questions about this BAC Calculator, blood alcohol content, the Widmark formula, and responsible drinking.

items.q: How accurate is this BAC Calculator?
items.a: This BAC Calculator uses the Widmark formula, a standard BAC estimation method. It is still only an estimate because food intake, hydration, medications, and metabolism can change real BAC.

items.q: What is the Widmark formula used in this BAC Calculator?
items.a: The Widmark formula estimates blood alcohol content from alcohol consumed, body weight, a distribution constant, and elapsed time. This BAC Calculator uses that formula because it remains the most common practical model for educational blood alcohol content estimation.

items.q: How long does it take for BAC to return to zero?
items.a: A common average metabolism rate is 0.015% BAC per hour. Coffee, water, and cold showers do not speed this up. Only time lowers BAC.

items.q: What counts as one standard drink?
items.a: A standard US drink contains 0.6 fluid ounces or about 14 grams of pure alcohol. That is about one 12 oz beer, one 5 oz glass of wine, or one 1.5 oz shot of spirits.

items.q: Why does biological sex affect the result?
items.a: The Widmark formula uses different distribution constants because body composition and alcohol distribution differ on average between males and females.

items.q: Does eating food lower my BAC?
items.a: Food does not lower BAC already in your bloodstream, but it can slow alcohol absorption and reduce peak BAC.

items.q: Can I use the BAC Calculator to decide if I am safe to drive?
items.a: No. This BAC Calculator is for education only. Never use an app to decide whether to drive after drinking.

legal.title: Legal BAC Limits by State and Country
legal.description: The legal BAC limit for driving varies by jurisdiction and driver category. This BAC Calculator compares your BAC estimate with common thresholds, but a legal BAC does not mean you are safe to drive.
legal.note: Always verify the current legal BAC limit with your local authority before driving. Laws can change and often vary by age, license type, and prior offenses.

references.title: Why This BAC Calculator Uses These Inputs
references.description: This BAC Calculator explains its estimate through standard drink conversion, Widmark formula modeling, and the commonly cited average metabolism rate used in educational tools.

items.title: Widmark Formula
items.body: This BAC Calculator uses the Widmark formula because it remains one of the most widely recognized methods for estimating blood alcohol content. In practical form, the formula is BAC = (A / (W x r)) x 100 - (0.015 x T), where A is pure alcohol in grams, W is body weight in grams, r is the body-water distribution constant, and T is hours elapsed. The r value is commonly modeled as 0.68 for males and 0.55 for females to reflect average alcohol distribution differences. Erik Widmark developed this approach in the 1930s, and it still underpins many forensic and educational blood alcohol content calculations.

items.title: Standard Drink Conversion
items.body: A BAC Calculator is only as useful as its drink assumptions, so drink type matters. One standard US drink contains about 0.6 fluid ounces or 14 grams of pure alcohol, which is roughly a 12 oz beer at 5% ABV, a 5 oz glass of wine at 12% ABV, or a 1.5 oz pour of 40% spirits. Strong beer can contain substantially more alcohol than regular beer, which is why a strong beer entry in the BAC Calculator can produce a noticeably higher blood alcohol content estimate from the same drink count.

items.title: Average Metabolism Rate
items.body: This BAC Calculator applies the commonly cited average decline rate of about 0.015% BAC per hour to estimate how long alcohol may take to clear. Real metabolism can vary meaningfully, and published ranges often fall closer to 0.010% to 0.020% BAC per hour depending on liver health, genetics, food, and other biological factors. Coffee, water, exercise, and cold showers do not accelerate alcohol metabolism once alcohol is already in the bloodstream. Only time reduces BAC in a reliable way.

blog.title: BAC and blood alcohol content explained clearly.
blog.description: Research-backed articles about how BAC calculators work, legal driving thresholds, and the science of alcohol metabolism.

how.title: How BAC Is Calculated: The Widmark Formula Explained
how.intro: Every online BAC calculator is built on the same foundation: the Widmark formula. Understanding how that formula works helps you interpret estimates more realistically and see where the model has limits.
formulaItems: A is total alcohol consumed in grams.
W is body weight in grams.
r is the Widmark distribution constant.
T is hours elapsed since drinking began.
how.distributionBody: The distribution constant estimates how alcohol disperses through body water. Because body composition differs on average, the standard Widmark model uses different constants for males and females.
how.conversionBody: A standard US drink contains about 14 grams of pure alcohol. BAC calculators first convert drink count and drink type into grams of alcohol before applying the formula.
how.metabolismBody: The liver metabolizes alcohol over time, and a commonly used average is 0.015% BAC per hour. That is why only time lowers BAC in a meaningful way.
how.limitsBody: Food, medication, hydration, genetics, and drink size variation can all move actual BAC away from the estimate. Treat any calculator as a guide for awareness, not a precise measurement.

legalLimits.title: Legal BAC Limits by State: A Complete 2025 Guide
legalLimits.intro: Understanding legal BAC limits helps frame the risk of driving after drinking. In most of the United States, the standard limit for adult drivers is 0.08%, with stricter rules for Utah, commercial drivers, and people under 21.
legalLimits.federalBody: All states now use 0.08% as the general limit for drivers 21 and over, except Utah, which lowered its limit to 0.05% in 2019.
legalLimits.under21Body: Under-21 limits are commonly 0.00% to 0.02%, which means a single drink can create legal risk even when an adult driver might still be under the general 0.08% threshold.
legalLimits.cdlBody: CDL drivers are held to a stricter 0.04% standard in every state, reflecting the increased safety requirements for commercial vehicles.

comparison.title: BAC Calculator vs Breathalyzer: Which Is More Accurate?
comparison.intro: BAC calculators, breathalyzers, and blood tests all answer similar questions in different ways. They vary significantly in accuracy, cost, convenience, and legal reliability.
comparison.calculatorBody: A calculator only knows the information you enter. It cannot measure alcohol in your breath or blood, so it must assume average metabolism rates and standard drink sizes.
comparison.breathalyzerBody: Personal breathalyzers usually provide a better estimate than a calculator, but calibration, breathing pattern, and recent alcohol exposure in the mouth can still affect readings.
comparison.bloodBody: Laboratory blood tests are the most accurate measurement and carry the strongest legal weight, but they are not instant and are not practical for casual use.
